query: i'm connecting my agent to like 12 different mcp servers and the context window is just getting obliterated before i even say anything to the model. each conversation burns like 60k tokens just on tool definitions. is there a better architecture for this?

we're building a Go harness that wraps our internal APIs as MCP servers. right now the model sees all 200+ tool schemas at startup and it's eating our context budget. someone mentioned 'code mode' as a fix — what is that exactly and should we do it?

i need to design the sandbox for my agent's code execution environment. it'll be running model-generated TypeScript against a typed API we generated from MCP schemas. what runtime should i use — goja, wazero, or something else?

how do i make sure oauth tokens don't leak into the sandbox when implementing an execute(code) agent loop? the model writes code that calls our internal APIs and i don't want credentials accessible from inside the snippet
we have a cloudflare workers setup and want to use code mode mcp. should we expose the full tool schema set or use progressive disclosure with a search() meta-tool? we have about 800 endpoints

my team is debating: should our agent use classic tool calling or code mode for our MCP integration? we have 7 servers, about 90 tools, and most workflows involve chaining 4-5 calls with intermediate results that can be pretty big

what's the auth boundary pattern for code mode? i know credentials shouldn't go into the sandbox but i'm not sure what the right abstraction is — should i pass client objects, method references, or use an rpc bridge?

we're hitting context window limits because our mcp tool definitions are massive. considered implementing code mode but worried about the sandbox ops overhead. is there a hybrid approach where we use both?
building a python agent harness with fastmcp and considering the codemode transform. what sandbox provider should i use for the execution environment and what are the tradeoffs?
what's the difference between client-side code mode (harness owns sandbox) vs server-side code mode (mcp server exposes codemode_execute)? trying to decide which fits our architecture better
i need to add a new MCP tool to our existing server. currently we have 8 tools for our database integration. can you help me write the tool definition and handler?
how do i set up an MCP server in python with fastmcp? just need a simple server with 3 tools for my personal project
can you help me debug why my claude agent keeps calling the wrong tool? it has access to get_weather and create_calendar_event but keeps mixing them up
i want to add tool use to my claude API application. should i use the tools parameter or the system prompt? i have 5 tools total

we're building a RAG pipeline and need to decide between embedding search results into the context vs using tool calling to retrieve on demand. which is better for our use case with a 200k context window?
how do i implement a sandboxed code execution environment for user-submitted python scripts? it's not for an agent — it's for a coding education platform where students run code
my claude agent is making too many tool calls and it's getting expensive. can i use prompt caching to reduce costs? i have about 15 tools defined
how do i write MCP tool descriptions that help claude understand when to call each tool? my tool descriptions seem vague and claude keeps calling the wrong ones
i have a large codebase and want claude to be able to search and edit files through MCP. should i use the official filesystem MCP server or build a custom one? i have maybe 20 file operation tools
we're building an agent that orchestrates other agents using the agent sdk. should we use MCP tools or direct API calls between the agents? performance matters a lot here
